i have these in my production dopplar but i need NEXT_PUBLIC_PRIVY_APP_ID?

Yes. For wallet connect in this app you need NEXT_PUBLIC_PRIVY_APP_ID — not just PRIVY_APP_ID.

Why
Next.js only exposes env vars to the browser when they start with NEXT_PUBLIC_.

The frontend reads:


Providers.tsx
Lines 21-21
const privyAppId = process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_PRIVY_APP_ID;
PRIVY_APP_ID (no prefix) stays server-side. The client never sees it, so Privy stays disabled and you get “set NEXT_PUBLIC_PRIVY_APP_ID”.

What to add in nuro-ethglobal / dev
Doppler secret	Value
NEXT_PUBLIC_PRIVY_APP_ID
Same value as prod PRIVY_APP_ID
That’s it for wallet connect in the hackathon tree.

Prod secrets you can skip locally (for now)
Secret	Used in hackathon code?
PRIVY_APP_SECRET
Not referenced in this repo
PRIVY_JWKS_ENDPOINT
Not referenced in this repo
Copy them if you want parity with prod; they’re not required for Uniswap swap signing via Privy wallet in the browser.

After adding
Restart the FE dev server — Next bakes NEXT_PUBLIC_* at startup:

# kill and restart
pnpm dev
Same App ID from Privy dashboard → Settings → App ID. Dev and prod can share the same app ID for local testing.

i have privy handling wallet connect and google oauth do i need to port over my google secrets into the ethglobal dopplar as well ? 

Short answer: Privy covers wallet connect. It does not replace Google on the login page in this codebase today.

Two separate flows
Flow	What handles it	Doppler keys
Wallet connect (Reload, swap signing)
Privy
NEXT_PUBLIC_PRIVY_APP_ID only
“Sign in with Google” on /login
NextAuth (not Privy)
GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID, GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET
The login page still calls signIn("google") via NextAuth. SocialLoginSection / Privy Google exists but isn’t wired into LoginForm — so the Google button does not go through Privy.

Backend /auth/social-login also verifies Google tokens against GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID — that’s the NextAuth Google client ID, not Privy.

Do you need Google secrets in ethglobal Doppler?
Skip Google if your hackathon path is:

Log in with email/password (or demo account + NURO_LOCAL_DEV_OTP)
Use Privy only for wallet on Reload / Uniswap
Keep Google if you want the Google button on the login page to work locally.

You already uploaded .env.local earlier — GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID and GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET are probably already in nuro-ethglobal / dev. No need to copy again from prod unless you deleted them.

Minimum for hackathon (no Google login)
NEXT_PUBLIC_PRIVY_APP_ID
AUTH_SECRET          # or NEXTAUTH_SECRET
NEXTAUTH_URL         # http://localhost:2800
CASHLY_API_URL       # http://localhost:3000
BACKEND_URL          # http://localhost:3000
JWT_SECRET           # backend
POSTGRES_URL / DATABASE_URL
Bottom line
Wallet: Privy only → you’re set with NEXT_PUBLIC_PRIVY_APP_ID.
Google login button: still NextAuth → need Google secrets, or don’t use that button.
Prod PRIVY_APP_SECRET / PRIVY_JWKS_ENDPOINT: not used in this repo for wallet connect — skip for ethglobal unless you add server-side Privy verification later.

Good on Google rotation and Uniswap. Here’s what you still need for World and Arc — and what you don’t.

World ID (IDKit) — register
Where: developer.worldcoin.org (World Developer Portal)

Steps:

Create an app (Continuity / web integration — IDKit, not MiniKit-as-primary)
Enable World ID / create a Relying Party
Create an action (e.g. nuro-reload-verify or reload-gate)
Copy credentials into nuro-ethglobal Doppler:
Doppler key	From portal
NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_ID
App ID (app_...)
RP_ID
Relying Party ID (rp_...)
RP_SIGNING_KEY
RP signing key (hex)
No separate World “API key” — backend verification hits developer.world.org/api/v4/verify/{RP_ID} using those three values (see the World template’s verify-proof + rp-signature routes).

Optional for local web testing: ngrok + AUTH_URL only if you copy the MiniKit auth path. For IDKit gate on Reload in a normal browser, you typically don’t need that.

For judges: They’ll need World App (or supported web flow) to complete verification once — mention that in the demo video.

Arc (Circle) — mostly no signup
For your thin DepositSplitter on Arc testnet prototype, you do not need a Circle Developer API key.

Need	How
Testnet USDC (gas)
faucet.circle.com → Arc Testnet
RPC
Public: https://rpc.testnet.arc.network
Chain ID
5042002
USDC ERC-20 (6 dec)
0x3600000000000000000000000000000000000000
Deploy wallet
Throwaway testnet key in Doppler (e.g. ARC_DEPLOYER_PRIVATE_KEY) — not prod PRIVATE_KEY
Add to Doppler (when you wire Arc):

ARC_RPC_URL=https://rpc.testnet.arc.network
ARC_CHAIN_ID=5042002
ARC_USDC_ADDRESS=0x3600000000000000000000000000000000000000
Skip for your Arc prize lane: Nanopayments / x402 / Dynamic / Unlink — that’s a different bounty.

http://localhost:2800 doesnt take it 

World rejects localhost — it wants a public HTTPS URL. Two ways to unblock:

Option A — ngrok (fastest for tonight)
# Terminal 1 — FE already running on 2800
pnpm dev
# Terminal 2
ngrok http 2800
Copy the https URL ngrok gives you (e.g. https://abc123.ngrok-free.app).

Paste that into World App URL → create app → grab keys.

Note: Free ngrok URLs change when you restart ngrok. Update World App URL when it changes, or use a fixed ngrok domain if you have one.

If you don’t have ngrok:

brew install ngrok
# or download from ngrok.com, sign up free, run: ngrok config add-authtoken YOUR_TOKEN
Option B — Vercel preview (stable URL)
Push hackathon branch to GitHub (or connect Vercel to the worktree repo)
Deploy → get https://nuro-ethglobal-xxx.vercel.app
Use that in World App URL
Later add ethglobal.nuro.finance in GoDaddy → point at Vercel
Better for judges; takes a bit longer than ngrok.

What won’t work
http://localhost:2800 ❌
http://127.0.0.1:2800 ❌
https://ethglobal.nuro.finance ❌ until DNS + deploy + SSL are live
GoDaddy alone doesn’t fix it — something must answer on HTTPS at that hostname.

the json has the public key is taht rp id? 

No. The public key in the JSON is not RP_ID.

They’re different things:

What	What it is	Doppler name
Private key (in JSON)
Signs proof requests
RP_SIGNING_KEY
Public key (in JSON)
Matches that private key — World already has it from Managed setup
Don’t put in Doppler (you don’t use it in the app)
RP_ID
Portal-assigned Relying Party id (rp_... or similar)
RP_ID
App ID
Your app id (app_...)
NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_ID
Where to get RP_ID: Developer Portal → your app → World ID section (after you finished Managed setup). It’s labeled RP ID / Relying Party ID — not in the key JSON.

Where to get NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_ID: Same app → Overview / Settings → App ID (app_...).

JSON → Doppler: only the private key → RP_SIGNING_KEY. Ignore the public key for env vars.

  "result": "{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"### IDKitWidget Component for World ID Verification\\n\\nSource: https://context7.com/worldcoin/idkit-js/llms.txt\\n\\nThis React component renders a modal dialog for World ID verification, managing the UI and state. It requires the &#96;@worldcoin/idkit&#96; library and uses a callback function to handle the verification result by sending the proof to a backend API.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;jsx\\nimport { IDKitWidget, VerificationLevel, ISuccessResult } from '@worldcoin/idkit'\\n\\nfunction App() {\\n  const handleVerify = async (proof) =\u003e {\\n    const response = await fetch('/api/verify', {\\n      method: 'POST',\\n      headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },\\n      body: JSON.stringify({\\n        proof,\\n        app_id: 'app_b43756ba8253a1b97e8ce4bc41c261a0',\\n        action: 'login',\\n        signal: 'user_id_123'\\n      })\\n    })\\n\\n    if (!response.ok) {\\n      throw new Error('Verification failed')\\n    }\\n  }\\n\\n  return (\\n    \u003cIDKitWidget\\n      app_id=\\\"app_b43756ba8253a1b97e8ce4bc41c261a0\\\"\\n      action=\\\"login\\\"\\n      signal=\\\"user_id_123\\\"\\n      verification_level={VerificationLevel.Device}\\n      onSuccess={(result) =\u003e console.log('Verified:', result)}\\n      handleVerify={handleVerify}\\n      onError={(error) =\u003e console.error('Error:', error)}\\n    \u003e\\n      {({ open }) =\u003e (\\n        \u003cbutton onClick={open}\u003eVerify with World ID\u003c/button\u003e\\n      )}\\n    \u003c/IDKitWidget\u003e\\n  )\\n}\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Verify Cloud Proofs with Worldcoin API\\n\\nSource: https://context7.com/worldcoin/idkit-js/llms.txt\\n\\nThis backend function validates World ID proofs using the &#96;verifyCloudProof&#96; function from the &#96;@worldcoin/idkit&#96; library. It takes proof details from the request body and returns a success or error response based on the Worldcoin verification API's result.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;typescript\\nimport { verifyCloudProof, ISuccessResult } from '@worldcoin/idkit'\\n\\nasync function handler(req, res) {\\n  const { proof, app_id, action, signal } = req.body\\n\\n  const verificationResponse = await verifyCloudProof(\\n    proof,\\n    app_id,\\n    action,\\n    signal\\n  )\\n\\n  if (verificationResponse.success) {\\n    // Proof is valid - proceed with authentication\\n    return res.status(200).json({ verified: true })\\n  } else {\\n    // Invalid proof\\n    return res.status(400).json({\\n      error: verificationResponse.detail,\\n      code: verificationResponse.code\\n    })\\n  }\\n}\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Use IDKit Widget in React\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/worldcoin/idkit-js/blob/main/README.md\\n\\nDemonstrates the basic usage of the IDKitWidget in a React application. It requires an actionId, signal, and a handleVerify callback function. The widget renders a button that, when clicked, opens the World ID verification modal.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;jsx\\nimport { IDKitWidget } from \\\"@worldcoin/idkit\\\";\\n\\n\u003cIDKitWidget actionId=\\\"get_this_from_the_dev_portal\\\" signal=\\\"my_signal\\\" handleVerify={verifyProof}\u003e\\n  {({ open }) =\u003e (\\n    {/* You can render whatever you want here, and call open() to open the widget */}\\n    \u003cbutton onClick={open}\u003eVerify with World ID\u003c/button\u003e\\n  )}\\n\u003c/IDKitWidget\u003e\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Standalone World ID Verification with Vanilla JavaScript\\n\\nSource: https://context7.com/worldcoin/idkit-js/llms.txt\\n\\nEnables World ID verification in web applications using vanilla JavaScript, without React dependencies. It utilizes the &#96;@worldcoin/idkit-standalone&#96; library, allowing initialization and control of the verification modal directly through HTML buttons and JavaScript functions.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;html\\n\u003c!DOCTYPE html\u003e\\n\u003chtml\u003e\\n\u003chead\u003e\\n  \u003cscript src=\\\"https://unpkg.com/@worldcoin/idkit-standalone@latest/dist/idkit.min.js\\\"\u003e\u003c/script\u003e\\n\u003c/head\u003e\\n\u003cbody\u003e\\n  \u003cbutton onclick=\\\"verifyUser()\\\"\u003eVerify with World ID\u003c/button\u003e\\n\\n  \u003cscript\u003e\\n    // Initialize once on page load\\n    window.IDKit.init({\\n      app_id: 'app_b43756ba8253a1b97e8ce4bc41c261a0',\\n      action: 'login',\\n      signal: 'user_session_id',\\n      verification_level: 'device',\\n      onSuccess: (result) =\u003e {\\n        console.log('Verified:', result)\\n        fetch('/api/verify', {\\n          method: 'POST',\\n          headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },\\n          body: JSON.stringify(result)\\n        })\\n      },\\n      handleVerify: async (proof) =\u003e {\\n        const res = await fetch('/api/verify', {\\n          method: 'POST',\\n          body: JSON.stringify(proof)\\n        })\\n        if (!res.ok) throw new Error('Failed')\\n      },\\n      onError: (error) =\u003e {\\n        console.error('Error:', error)\\n      }\\n    })\\n\\n    function verifyUser() {\\n      window.IDKit.open()\\n    }\\n  \u003c/script\u003e\\n\u003c/body\u003e\\n\u003c/html\u003e\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Control IDKit Widget with useIDKit React Hook\\n\\nSource: https://context7.com/worldcoin/idkit-js/llms.txt\\n\\nProvides a React hook, &#96;useIDKit&#96;, for managing the IDKitWidget modal programmatically. It allows opening and closing the modal and defining callbacks for verification success and errors without using render props. This hook simplifies modal integration in React applications.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;tsx\\nimport { useIDKit } from '@worldcoin/idkit'\\n\\nfunction CustomButton() {\\n  const { open, setOpen } = useIDKit({\\n    handleVerify: async (proof) =\u003e {\\n      const res = await fetch('/api/verify', {\\n        method: 'POST',\\n        body: JSON.stringify(proof)\\n      })\\n      if (!res.ok) throw new Error('Verification failed')\\n    },\\n    onSuccess: (result) =\u003e {\\n      console.log('Success:', result.nullifier_hash)\\n    }\\n  })\\n\\n  return (\\n    \u003cdiv\u003e\\n      \u003cbutton onClick={() =\u003e setOpen(true)}\u003eStart Verification\u003c/button\u003e\\n      \u003cbutton onClick={() =\u003e setOpen(false)}\u003eCancel\u003c/button\u003e\\n    \u003c/div\u003e\\n  )\\n}\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\"}]}"
}

{
  "result": "{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"### Verify Proof on Backend\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/worldcoin/developer-docs/blob/main/world-id/idkit/integrate.mdx\\n\\nForward the IDKit result payload to the World ID Developer Portal API for verification. Ensure the &#96;rp_id&#96; is correctly included in the URL. The payload should be sent as-is.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;typescript\\nimport {\\n\\tNextResponse\\n} from \\\"next/server\\\";\\nimport type { IDKitResult } from \\\"@worldcoin/idkit\\\";\\n\\nexport async function POST(request: Request): Promise\u003cResponse\u003e {\\n\\tconst { rp_id, idkitResponse } = (await request.json()) as {\\n\\t\\trp_id: string;\\n\\t\\tidkitResponse: IDKitResult;\\n\\t};\\n\\n\\tconst response = await fetch(\\n\\t\\t&#96;https://developer.world.org/api/v4/verify/${rp_id}&#96;,\\n\\t\\t{\\n\\t\\t\\tmethod: \\\"POST\\\",\\n\\t\\t\\theaders: { \\\"content-type\\\": \\\"application/json\\\" },\\n\\t\\t\\tbody: JSON.stringify(idkitResponse),\\n\\t\\t}\\n\\t);\\n\\n\\tif (!response.ok) {\\n\\t\\treturn NextResponse.json({ error: \\\"Verification failed\\\" }, { status: 400 });\\n\\t}\\n\\n\\t// Proof is valid — now store the nullifier (see Step 6)\\n\\treturn NextResponse.json({ success: true });\\n}\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Server-Side Proof Verification (v4)\\n\\nSource: https://context7.com/worldcoin/developer-docs/llms.txt\\n\\nThis Next.js API route forwards the IDKit result payload to the Developer Portal for verification. Store the returned nullifier hash to prevent double-verification. Handles JSON request and response.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;typescript\\n// app/api/verify-proof/route.ts\\nimport { NextResponse } from \\\"next/server\\\";\\nimport type { IDKitResult } from \\\"@worldcoin/idkit\\\";\\n\\nexport async function POST(request: Request): Promise\u003cResponse\u003e {\\n  const { rp_id, idkitResponse } = (await request.json()) as {\\n    rp_id: string;\\n    idkitResponse: IDKitResult;\\n  };\\n\\n  // Forward the payload directly — no field remapping needed\\n  const response = await fetch(\\n    &#96;https://developer.world.org/api/v4/verify/${rp_id}&#96;,\\n    {\\n      method: \\\"POST\\\",\\n      headers: { \\\"content-type\\\": \\\"application/json\\\" },\\n      body: JSON.stringify(idkitResponse),\\n    }\\n  );\\n\\n  if (!response.ok) {\\n    const err = await response.json();\\n    return NextResponse.json({ error: err }, { status: 400 });\\n  }\\n\\n  const data = await response.json();\\n  // data.nullifier_hash — store this to enforce one-human-one-action\\n  // Convert hex nullifier to NUMERIC(78,0) for PostgreSQL\\n  const nullifier = BigInt(data.nullifier_hash).toString(10);\\n\\n  // Example: INSERT INTO nullifiers (nullifier, action) VALUES ($1, $2)\\n  //          ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING — reject if already exists\\n  return NextResponse.json({ success: true, nullifier });\\n}\\n\\n/*\\nPostgreSQL schema for nullifier storage:\\nCREATE TABLE nullifiers (\\n  nullifier   NUMERIC(78, 0) NOT NULL,\\n  action      TEXT NOT NULL,\\n  verified_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),\\n  UNIQUE (nullifier, action)\\n);\\n*/\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### POST /api/v4/verify/{rp_id}\\n\\nSource: https://context7.com/worldcoin/developer-docs/llms.txt\\n\\nServer-side endpoint to verify World ID proofs. It forwards the IDKit result payload to the Developer Portal for verification and returns the nullifier hash upon success.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;APIDOC\\n## POST /api/v4/verify/{rp_id}\\n\\n### Description\\nForward the raw IDKit result payload as-is to the Developer Portal. Store the nullifier after success to prevent double-verification.\\n\\n### Method\\nPOST\\n\\n### Endpoint\\n&#96;/api/v4/verify/{rp_id}&#96;\\n\\n### Parameters\\n#### Path Parameters\\n- **rp_id** (string) - Required - The Relying Party ID.\\n\\n#### Request Body\\n- **rp_id** (string) - Required - The Relying Party ID.\\n- **idkitResponse** (object) - Required - The raw IDKit result payload.\\n  - **merkle_root** (string) - Required - The Merkle root of the World ID state.\\n  - **nullifier_hash** (string) - Required - The nullifier hash of the proof.\\n  - **proof** (string) - Required - The zero-knowledge proof.\\n  - **verification_level** (string) - Required - The verification level ('orb' or 'phone').\\n  - **action** (string) - Required - The action identifier.\\n  - **signal_hash** (string) - Optional - The hash of the signal.\\n  - **max_age** (number) - Optional - Maximum age of the proof in seconds.\\n\\n### Response\\n#### Success Response (200)\\n- **success** (boolean) - Indicates if the verification was successful.\\n- **nullifier** (string) - The verified nullifier hash, converted to a string.\\n\\n#### Error Response (400)\\n- **error** (object) - Details about the verification error.\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n### Step 5: Verify the proof in your backend\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/worldcoin/developer-docs/blob/main/world-id/idkit/integrate.mdx\\n\\nAfter a user completes the verification flow, IDKit returns a response payload. This payload should be forwarded as-is to your backend and then sent directly to the &#96;POST https://developer.world.org/api/v4/verify/{rp_id}&#96; endpoint for verification.\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### POST /api/v4/verify/{rp_id} — server-side proof verification\\n\\nSource: https://context7.com/worldcoin/developer-docs/llms.txt\\n\\nThe &#96;POST /api/v4/verify/{rp_id}&#96; endpoint is used for server-side verification of World ID proofs. You should forward the raw IDKit result payload to this endpoint. After a successful verification, it's crucial to store the returned nullifier to prevent duplicate verifications for the same action.\"}]}"
}

  "result": "{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"### Server-side IDKit Helpers: signRequest and hashSignal (TypeScript)\\n\\nSource: https://context7.com/worldcoin/developer-docs/llms.txt\\n\\nUse these low-level utilities exclusively in trusted server environments. &#96;signRequest&#96; generates a signed RP context, while &#96;hashSignal&#96; hashes user identifiers before binding them to a proof.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;typescript\\nimport { signRequest } from \\\"@worldcoin/idkit-core/signing\\\";\\\\nimport { hashSignal } from \\\"@worldcoin/idkit-core/hashing\\\";\\\\n\\\\n// Generate a signed RP context — run only on your backend\\\\nconst { sig, nonce, createdAt, expiresAt } = signRequest({\\\\n  signingKeyHex: process.env.RP_SIGNING_KEY!, // hex string from Developer Portal\\\\n  action: \\\"my-action\\\",\\\\n  ttl: 300, // optional, seconds until expiry (default: 300)\\\\n});\\\\n\\\\nconsole.log({ sig, nonce, createdAt, expiresAt });\\\\n// {\\\\n//   sig:       \\\"0xabcdef...\\\",\\\\n//   nonce:     \\\"0x1a2b3c...\\\",\\\\n//   createdAt: 1735689600,\\\\n//   expiresAt: 1735689900\\\\n// }\\\\n\\\\n// Hash a signal (e.g., user ID or wallet address) before binding it to a proof\\\\nconst signalHash = hashSignal(\\\"user-wallet-0xabc123\\\");\\\\n// Returns: \\\"0x00c5d246...\\\" — 32-byte keccak256 hash\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Generate RP Signature Backend\\n\\nSource: https://context7.com/worldcoin/developer-docs/llms.txt\\n\\nBackend code to generate a signed request for IDKit. Ensure your RP_SIGNING_KEY is kept secret and never exposed to the client.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;typescript\\nimport {\\n  NextResponse\\n} from \\\"next/server\\\";\\nimport { signRequest } from \\\"@worldcoin/idkit-core/signing\\\";\\n\\nexport async function POST(request: Request): Promise\u003cResponse\u003e {\\n  const { action } = await request.json();\\n\\n  const { sig, nonce, createdAt, expiresAt } = signRequest({\\n    signingKeyHex: process.env.RP_SIGNING_KEY!, // from Developer Portal — never expose\\n    action,\\n    ttl: 300, // optional, default 300s\\n  });\\n\\n  return NextResponse.json({\\n    sig,\\n    nonce,\\n    created_at: createdAt,\\n    expires_at: expiresAt,\\n  });\\n}\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Generate RP Signature (JavaScript)\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/worldcoin/developer-docs/blob/main/world-id/idkit/integrate.mdx\\n\\nUse the &#96;signRequest&#96; function from &#96;@worldcoin/idkit-core/signing&#96; to generate a signature for World ID proof requests on your backend. Ensure your RP signing key is stored securely as an environment variable.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;typescript\\nimport { NextResponse } from \\\"next/server\\\";\\nimport { signRequest } from \\\"@worldcoin/idkit-core/signing\\\";\\n\\nexport async function POST(request: Request): Promise\u003cResponse\u003e {\\n  const { action } = await request.json();\\n\\n  const { sig, nonce, createdAt, expiresAt } = signRequest({\\n    signingKeyHex: process.env.RP_SIGNING_KEY!,\\n    action,\\n  });\\n\\n  return NextResponse.json({\\n    sig,\\n    nonce,\\n    created_at: createdAt,\\n    expires_at: expiresAt,\\n  });\\n}\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Request IDKit Proof and Collect Response\\n\\nSource: https://context7.com/worldcoin/developer-docs/llms.txt\\n\\nClient-side code to request an IDKit proof, display a QR code, and poll for verification completion. Requires fetching an RP signature from the backend first.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;typescript\\nimport { IDKit, orbLegacy } from \\\"@worldcoin/idkit-core\\\";\\n\\n// 1. Fetch RP signature from your backend\\nconst rpSig = await fetch(\\\"/api/rp-signature\\\", {\\n  method: \\\"POST\\\",\\n  headers: { \\\"content-type\\\": \\\"application/json\\\" },\\n  body: JSON.stringify({ action: \\\"claim-airdrop-2026\\\" }),\\n}).then((r) =\u003e r.json());\\n\\n// 2. Create the IDKit request\\nconst request = await IDKit.request({\\n  app_id: \\\"app_staging_xxxxx\\\",          // from Developer Portal\\n  action: \\\"claim-airdrop-2026\\\",          // scopes the nullifier\\n  rp_context: {\\n    rp_id: \\\"rp_xxxxx\\\",                   // from Developer Portal\\n    nonce: rpSig.nonce,\\n    created_at: rpSig.created_at,\\n    expires_at: rpSig.expires_at,\\n    signature: rpSig.sig,\\n  },\\n  allow_legacy_proofs: true,\\n  environment: \\\"production\\\",             // use \\\"staging\\\" for the simulator\\n  return_to: \\\"myapp://verify-done\\\",      // optional mobile deep-link\\n}).preset(orbLegacy({ signal: \\\"wallet-0xabc123\\\" })); // optional signal\\n\\n// 3. Show QR code using connectorURI, then poll\\nconst connectUrl = request.connectorURI; // render this as a QR code\\n\\nconst completion = await request.pollUntilCompletion({\\n  pollInterval: 2_000,\\n  timeout: 120_000,\\n});\\n\\nif (!completion.success) {\\n  console.error(\\\"Verification failed or timed out:\\\", completion.error);\\n} else {\\n  console.log(\\\"Proof payload:\\\", completion.result);\\n  // Forward completion.result to your backend for verification\\n}\\n\\n/*\\nExample IDKit response payload (World ID 4.0 Uniqueness):\\n{\\n  \\\"protocol_version\\\": \\\"4.0\\\",\\n  \\\"nonce\\\": \\\"a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890\\\",\\n  \\\"action\\\": \\\"claim-airdrop-2026\\\",\\n  \\\"environment\\\": \\\"production\\\",\\n  \\\"responses\\\": [\\n    {\\n      \\\"identifier\\\": \\\"orb\\\",\\n      \\\"signal_hash\\\": \\\"0x0\\\",\\n      \\\"proof\\\": [\\\"0x1a2b...\\\", \\\"0x3c4d...\\\", \\\"0x5e6f...\\\", \\\"0x7a8b...\\\", \\\"0x9c0d...\\\"],\\n      \\\"nullifier\\\": \\\"0x04e5f6...rp_scoped_nullifier\\\",\\n      \\\"issuer_schema_id\\\": 1,\\n      \\\"expires_at_min\\\": 1756166400\\n    }\\n  ]\\n}\\n*/\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Sign RP Requests in Go\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/worldcoin/developer-docs/blob/main/world-id/idkit/signatures.mdx\\n\\nThe &#96;idkit&#96; Go package provides functions for signing RP requests. You can use &#96;SignRequest&#96; directly or create a reusable &#96;Signer&#96; for high-throughput backends. Ensure your signing key is securely managed.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;go\\nimport \\\"github.com/worldcoin/idkit/go/idkit\\\"\\n\\n// One-shot signing with options\\nsig, err := idkit.SignRequest(\\n  os.Getenv(\\\"RP_SIGNING_KEY\\\"),\\n  idkit.WithAction(\\\"my-action\\\"),\\n  idkit.WithTTL(300), // optional, default 300s\\n)\\n\\n// Or create a reusable signer for high-throughput backends\\nsigner, err := idkit.NewSigner(os.Getenv(\\\"RP_SIGNING_KEY\\\"))\\nsig, err = signer.SignRequest(idkit.WithAction(\\\"my-action\\\"))\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\"}]}"
}

  "result": "{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"### IDKitWidget Component for World ID Verification\\n\\nSource: https://context7.com/worldcoin/idkit-js/llms.txt\\n\\nThis React component renders a modal dialog for World ID verification, managing the UI and state. It requires the &#96;@worldcoin/idkit&#96; library and uses a callback function to handle the verification result by sending the proof to a backend API.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;jsx\\nimport { IDKitWidget, VerificationLevel, ISuccessResult } from '@worldcoin/idkit'\\n\\nfunction App() {\\n  const handleVerify = async (proof) =\u003e {\\n    const response = await fetch('/api/verify', {\\n      method: 'POST',\\n      headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },\\n      body: JSON.stringify({\\n        proof,\\n        app_id: 'app_b43756ba8253a1b97e8ce4bc41c261a0',\\n        action: 'login',\\n        signal: 'user_id_123'\\n      })\\n    })\\n\\n    if (!response.ok) {\\n      throw new Error('Verification failed')\\n    }\\n  }\\n\\n  return (\\n    \u003cIDKitWidget\\n      app_id=\\\"app_b43756ba8253a1b97e8ce4bc41c261a0\\\"\\n      action=\\\"login\\\"\\n      signal=\\\"user_id_123\\\"\\n      verification_level={VerificationLevel.Device}\\n      onSuccess={(result) =\u003e console.log('Verified:', result)}\\n      handleVerify={handleVerify}\\n      onError={(error) =\u003e console.error('Error:', error)}\\n    \u003e\\n      {({ open }) =\u003e (\\n        \u003cbutton onClick={open}\u003eVerify with World ID\u003c/button\u003e\\n      )}\\n    \u003c/IDKitWidget\u003e\\n  )\\n}\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Configure Verification Levels with TypeScript\\n\\nSource: https://context7.com/worldcoin/idkit-js/llms.txt\\n\\nDemonstrates how to configure different verification levels (Orb, Device, SecureDocument) using the VerificationLevel enum from '@worldcoin/idkit'. Each level has specific security requirements and use cases.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;typescript\\nimport { VerificationLevel } from '@worldcoin/idkit'\\n\\n// Orb - highest security, requires biometric verification at Worldcoin Orb\\nconst orbConfig = {\\n  app_id: 'app_...',\\n  action: 'high-value-transaction',\\n  verification_level: VerificationLevel.Orb\\n}\\n\\n// Device - standard security, phone-based verification\\nconst deviceConfig = {\\n  app_id: 'app_...',\\n  action: 'user-registration',\\n  verification_level: VerificationLevel.Device\\n}\\n\\n// SecureDocument - government ID with liveness check\\nconst docConfig = {\\n  app_id: 'app_...',\\n  action: 'kyc-verification',\\n  verification_level: VerificationLevel.SecureDocument\\n}\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Initialize and Open IDKit JS SDK\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/worldcoin/idkit-js/blob/main/examples/with-html/index.html\\n\\nInitializes the IDKit SDK with essential parameters such as signal, app_id, action, and verification level. It also sets up an event listener to open the IDKit modal when a button is clicked. The &#96;handleVerify&#96; and &#96;onSuccess&#96; callbacks are crucial for processing the verification results.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;javascript\\nimport '@worldcoin/idkit-standalone'\\n\\nIDKit.init({\\n  signal: 'test_signal',\\n  app_id: 'app_ce4cb73cb75fc3b73b71ffb4de178410',\\n  action: 'test-action',\\n  action_description: 'Test action description',\\n  show_modal: true,\\n  container_id: 'idkit-container',\\n  partner: true,\\n  verification_level: 'orb',\\n  disable_default_modal_behavior: true,\\n  handleVerify: response =\u003e {\\n    // verify the IDKIt proof, throw an error to show the error screen\\n  },\\n  onSuccess: response =\u003e {\\n    console.log(response)\\n  },\\n});\\n\\ndocument.getElementById('open-idkit').addEventListener('click', async () =\u003e {\\n  console.log(IDKit.isInitialized)\\n  await IDKit.open()\\n});\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Control IDKit Widget with useIDKit React Hook\\n\\nSource: https://context7.com/worldcoin/idkit-js/llms.txt\\n\\nProvides a React hook, &#96;useIDKit&#96;, for managing the IDKitWidget modal programmatically. It allows opening and closing the modal and defining callbacks for verification success and errors without using render props. This hook simplifies modal integration in React applications.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;tsx\\nimport { useIDKit } from '@worldcoin/idkit'\\n\\nfunction CustomButton() {\\n  const { open, setOpen } = useIDKit({\\n    handleVerify: async (proof) =\u003e {\\n      const res = await fetch('/api/verify', {\\n        method: 'POST',\\n        body: JSON.stringify(proof)\\n      })\\n      if (!res.ok) throw new Error('Verification failed')\\n    },\\n    onSuccess: (result) =\u003e {\\n      console.log('Success:', result.nullifier_hash)\\n    }\\n  })\\n\\n  return (\\n    \u003cdiv\u003e\\n      \u003cbutton onClick={() =\u003e setOpen(true)}\u003eStart Verification\u003c/button\u003e\\n      \u003cbutton onClick={() =\u003e setOpen(false)}\u003eCancel\u003c/button\u003e\\n    \u003c/div\u003e\\n  )\\n}\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Standalone World ID Verification with Vanilla JavaScript\\n\\nSource: https://context7.com/worldcoin/idkit-js/llms.txt\\n\\nEnables World ID verification in web applications using vanilla JavaScript, without React dependencies. It utilizes the &#96;@worldcoin/idkit-standalone&#96; library, allowing initialization and control of the verification modal directly through HTML buttons and JavaScript functions.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;html\\n\u003c!DOCTYPE html\u003e\\n\u003chtml\u003e\\n\u003chead\u003e\\n  \u003cscript src=\\\"https://unpkg.com/@worldcoin/idkit-standalone@latest/dist/idkit.min.js\\\"\u003e\u003c/script\u003e\\n\u003c/head\u003e\\n\u003cbody\u003e\\n  \u003cbutton onclick=\\\"verifyUser()\\\"\u003eVerify with World ID\u003c/button\u003e\\n\\n  \u003cscript\u003e\\n    // Initialize once on page load\\n    window.IDKit.init({\\n      app_id: 'app_b43756ba8253a1b97e8ce4bc41c261a0',\\n      action: 'login',\\n      signal: 'user_session_id',\\n      verification_level: 'device',\\n      onSuccess: (result) =\u003e {\\n        console.log('Verified:', result)\\n        fetch('/api/verify', {\\n          method: 'POST',\\n          headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },\\n          body: JSON.stringify(result)\\n        })\\n      },\\n      handleVerify: async (proof) =\u003e {\\n        const res = await fetch('/api/verify', {\\n          method: 'POST',\\n          body: JSON.stringify(proof)\\n        })\\n        if (!res.ok) throw new Error('Failed')\\n      },\\n      onError: (error) =\u003e {\\n        console.error('Error:', error)\\n      }\\n    })\\n\\n    function verifyUser() {\\n      window.IDKit.open()\\n    }\\n  \u003c/script\u003e\\n\u003c/body\u003e\\n\u003c/html\u003e\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\"}]}"
}

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Reviewing Worldcoin developer documentation for the full React component with rp_context.

Checking Doppler for Worldcoin environment variables. Designing API routes for signature generation and proof verification. Building a WorldIdReloadGate component to wrap IDKit verification UI. Planning gate placement strategy.

Deciding between placing the World ID gate before amount selection or before the reload action. The cleanest UX keeps the current step layout but requires World verification before enabling the reload form.

Settled on a two-step gate pattern. The reload button on step 1 will open the World ID widget first. Verification succeeds before proceeding to step 2. Considering sessionStorage with a nullifier hash to cache verification state per session.

Defining environment variables: `WORLD_ACTION`, `NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_ID`, and `RP_ID`. Checking whether IDKit v4 handles `rp_context` fetching automatically or still requires manual backend signature requests.

Installing `@worldcoin/idkit` and `@worldcoin/idkit-core`. The `WorldIdReloadGate` component will wrap `IDKitWidget` and gate the `onNext` button in `ReloadOverview`. Drafting the `handleVerify` callback to post the IDKit result to `/api/world/verify-proof`.</details></think>

method to initiate a World ID proof request, including fetching the RP signature, configuring the request, and polling for completion.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;APIDOC\\n## IDKit.request()\\n\\n### Description\\nInitiates a World ID proof request, fetches the RP signature, and polls for verification completion.\\n\\n### Method\\n(Client-side JavaScript function)\\n\\n### Parameters\\n- **app_id** (string) - Required - Your application ID from the Developer Portal.\\n- **action** (string) - Required - An identifier that scopes the nullifier.\\n- **rp_context** (object) - Required - Context object containing RP details and signature.\\n  - **rp_id** (string) - Required - Your RP ID from the Developer Portal.\\n  - **nonce** (string) - Required - The nonce from the RP signature.\\n  - **created_at** (string) - Required - The creation timestamp of the RP signature.\\n  - **expires_at** (string) - Required - The expiration timestamp of the RP signature.\\n  - **signature** (string) - Required - The RP signature.\\n- **allow_legacy_proofs** (boolean) - Optional - Whether to allow legacy proofs.\\n- **environment** (string) - Required - The environment to use ('production' or 'staging').\\n- **return_to** (string) - Optional - A mobile deep-link to return to after verification.\\n- **preset** (function) - Optional - A preset function, e.g., &#96;orbLegacy({ signal: \\\"wallet-0xabc123\\\" })&#96;.\\n\\n### Usage\\n1. Fetch RP signature from your backend.\\n2. Call &#96;IDKit.request()&#96; with the necessary parameters.\\n3. Render the &#96;connectorURI&#96; as a QR code.\\n4. Poll &#96;request.pollUntilCompletion()&#96; for the verification result.\\n\\n### Example\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;javascript\\n// 1. Fetch RP signature\\nconst rpSig = await fetch(\\\"/api/rp-signature\\\", {\\n  method: \\\"POST\\\",\\n  headers: { \\\"content-type\\\": \\\"application/json\\\" },\\n  body: JSON.stringify({ action: \\\"claim-airdrop-2026\\\" }),\\n}).then((r) =\u003e r.json());\\n\\n// 2. Create the IDKit request\\nconst request = await IDKit.request({\\n  app_id: \\\"app_staging_xxxxx\\\",\\n  action: \\\"claim-airdrop-2026\\\",\\n  rp_context: {\\n    rp_id: \\\"rp_xxxxx\\\",\\n    nonce: rpSig.nonce,\\n    created_at: rpSig.created_at,\\n    expires_at: rpSig.expires_at,\\n    signature: rpSig.sig,\\n  },\\n  allow_legacy_proofs: true,\\n  environment: \\\"production\\\",\\n  return_to: \\\"myapp://verify-done\\\",\\n}).preset(orbLegacy({ signal: \\\"wallet-0xabc123\\\" }));\\n\\n// 3. Show QR code\\nconst connectUrl = request.connectorURI;\\n\\n// 4. Poll for completion\\nconst completion = await request.pollUntilCompletion({\\n  pollInterval: 2_000,\\n  timeout: 120_000,\\n});\\n\\nif (!completion.success) {\\n  console.error(\\\"Verification failed or timed out:\\\", completion.error);\\n} else {\\n  console.log(\\\"Proof payload:\\\", completion.result);\\n  // Forward completion.result to your backend for verification\\n}\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n### Response\\n- **connectorURI** (string) - The URI to be rendered as a QR code for initiating the verification.\\n- **pollUntilCompletion()** - A method that returns a promise resolving to a completion object.\\n  - **success** (boolean) - Indicates if the verification was successful.\\n  - **error** (string) - Error message if verification failed.\\n  - **result** (object) - The proof payload if verification was successful.\\n\\n### Example IDKit Response Payload (World ID 4.0 Uniqueness)\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;json\\n{\\n  \\\"protocol_version\\\": \\\"4.0\\\",\\n  \\\"nonce\\\": \\\"a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890\\\",\\n  \\\"action\\\": \\\"claim-airdrop-2026\\\",\\n  \\\"environment\\\": \\\"production\\\",\\n  \\\"responses\\\": [\\n    {\\n      \\\"identifier\\\": \\\"orb\\\",\\n      \\\"signal_hash\\\": \\\"0x0\\\",\\n      \\\"proof\\\": [\\\"0x1a2b...\\\", \\\"0x3c4d...\\\", \\\"0x5e6f...\\\", \\\"0x7a8b...\\\", \\\"0x9c0d...\\\"],\\n      \\\"nullifier\\\": \\\"0x04e5f6...rp_scoped_nullifier\\\",\\n      \\\"issuer_schema_id\\\": 1,\\n      \\\"expires_at_min\\\": 1756166400\\n    }\\n  ]\\n}\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Request IDKit Proof and Collect Response\\n\\nSource: https://context7.com/worldcoin/developer-docs/llms.txt\\n\\nClient-side code to request an IDKit proof, display a QR code, and poll for verification completion. Requires fetching an RP signature from the backend first.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;typescript\\nimport { IDKit, orbLegacy } from \\\"@worldcoin/idkit-core\\\";\\n\\n// 1. Fetch RP signature from your backend\\nconst rpSig = await fetch(\\\"/api/rp-signature\\\", {\\n  method: \\\"POST\\\",\\n  headers: { \\\"content-type\\\": \\\"application/json\\\" },\\n  body: JSON.stringify({ action: \\\"claim-airdrop-2026\\\" }),\\n}).then((r) =\u003e r.json());\\n\\n// 2. Create the IDKit request\\nconst request = await IDKit.request({\\n  app_id: \\\"app_staging_xxxxx\\\",          // from Developer Portal\\n  action: \\\"claim-airdrop-2026\\\",          // scopes the nullifier\\n  rp_context: {\\n    rp_id: \\\"rp_xxxxx\\\",                   // from Developer Portal\\n    nonce: rpSig.nonce,\\n    created_at: rpSig.created_at,\\n    expires_at: rpSig.expires_at,\\n    signature: rpSig.sig,\\n  },\\n  allow_legacy_proofs: true,\\n  environment: \\\"production\\\",             // use \\\"staging\\\" for the simulator\\n  return_to: \\\"myapp://verify-done\\\",      // optional mobile deep-link\\n}).preset(orbLegacy({ signal: \\\"wallet-0xabc123\\\" })); // optional signal\\n\\n// 3. Show QR code using connectorURI, then poll\\nconst connectUrl = request.connectorURI; // render this as a QR code\\n\\nconst completion = await request.pollUntilCompletion({\\n  pollInterval: 2_000,\\n  timeout: 120_000,\\n});\\n\\nif (!completion.success) {\\n  console.error(\\\"Verification failed or timed out:\\\", completion.error);\\n} else {\\n  console.log(\\\"Proof payload:\\\", completion.result);\\n  // Forward completion.result to your backend for verification\\n}\\n\\n/*\\nExample IDKit response payload (World ID 4.0 Uniqueness):\\n{\\n  \\\"protocol_version\\\": \\\"4.0\\\",\\n  \\\"nonce\\\": \\\"a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890\\\",\\n  \\\"action\\\": \\\"claim-airdrop-2026\\\",\\n  \\\"environment\\\": \\\"production\\\",\\n  \\\"responses\\\": [\\n    {\\n      \\\"identifier\\\": \\\"orb\\\",\\n      \\\"signal_hash\\\": \\\"0x0\\\",\\n      \\\"proof\\\": [\\\"0x1a2b...\\\", \\\"0x3c4d...\\\", \\\"0x5e6f...\\\", \\\"0x7a8b...\\\", \\\"0x9c0d...\\\"],\\n      \\\"nullifier\\\": \\\"0x04e5f6...rp_scoped_nullifier\\\",\\n      \\\"issuer_schema_id\\\": 1,\\n      \\\"expires_at_min\\\": 1756166400\\n    }\\n  ]\\n}\\n*/\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Server-side IDKit Helpers: signRequest and hashSignal (TypeScript)\\n\\nSource: https://context7.com/worldcoin/developer-docs/llms.txt\\n\\nUse these low-level utilities exclusively in trusted server environments. &#96;signRequest&#96; generates a signed RP context, while &#96;hashSignal&#96; hashes user identifiers before binding them to a proof.\\n\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;typescript\\nimport { signRequest } from \\\"@worldcoin/idkit-core/signing\\\";\\\\nimport { hashSignal } from \\\"@worldcoin/idkit-core/hashing\\\";\\\\n\\\\n// Generate a signed RP context — run only on your backend\\\\nconst { sig, nonce, createdAt, expiresAt } = signRequest({\\\\n  signingKeyHex: process.env.RP_SIGNING_KEY!, // hex string from Developer Portal\\\\n  action: \\\"my-action\\\",\\\\n  ttl: 300, // optional, seconds until expiry (default: 300)\\\\n});\\\\n\\\\nconsole.log({ sig, nonce, createdAt, expiresAt });\\\\n// {\\\\n//   sig:       \\\"0xabcdef...\\\",\\\\n//   nonce:     \\\"0x1a2b3c...\\\",\\\\n//   createdAt: 1735689600,\\\\n//   expiresAt: 1735689900\\\\n// }\\\\n\\\\n// Hash a signal (e.g., user ID or wallet address) before binding it to a proof\\\\nconst signalHash = hashSignal(\\\"user-wallet-0xabc123\\\");\\\\n// Returns: \\\"0x00c5d246...\\\" — 32-byte keccak256 hash\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\"}]}"

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Using `IDKitRequestWidget` from `@worldcoin/idkit` for v4 managed setup.

Deciding how to supply `rp_id` to the client. It is a public identifier, so exposing it via `NEXT_PUBLIC_RP_ID` is viable. Alternatively, the `/api/world/rp-signature` endpoint could return both `rp_id` and the signature.

Settled on returning `rp_id` from the signature endpoint to avoid an extra public environment variable. Will store it in component state.

The action name will be `NEXT_PUBLIC_WORLD_ACTION`, defaulting to `nuro-reload-verify`.

Creating `src/lib/world-id.ts` for constants, plus API routes for signature and proof verification, and a `WorldIdReloadGate` component.</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Beginning implementation now. A hard gate is in place.

Adding `@worldcoin/idkit` and `@worldcoin/idkit-core`. Creating the World ID utility, API routes for signature and proof verification, and the reload gate component. Updating `ReloadFlow.tsx` to integrate the gate and manage verification state.

The reload gate intercepts the CTA. Unverified users are sent to World ID verification instead of proceeding. The widget fetches the RP context when opened because the signature expires in five minutes. The component uses IDKitRequestWidget with environment variables for the app ID and action.

The gate component accepts open, onOpenChange, signal, and onVerified props. It tracks rpContext, rpId, and loadError states. When closed, it resets all state. It also validates the World ID app ID is configured.

The gate fetches the RP signature from the backend route when opened. It cancels stale requests and sets the RP ID and context once the response arrives.

The gate component now includes the signature and expiration timestamp in the RP context. It returns null when the World ID app ID is missing. The verify callback sends the proof result to the backend verification endpoint.

The verify callback forwards the IDKit result to the backend and throws on failure. The widget renders only after the RP context loads. It passes the legacy orb preset with the reload signal.

The gate component now triggers `onVerified()` and closes itself on successful verification. For ReloadOverview, the plan is to pass a `worldVerified` flag and an `onReloadClick` handler so the parent decides whether to open the World ID gate or proceed directly. The button will either launch the gate or call `onNext` depending on verification status.

The reload button is wired to `onReloadClick` and disabled when inactive. ReloadFlow introduces `worldVerified` and `worldGateOpen` state. The reload signal falls back to the user ID or email, defaulting to an anonymous identifier. Verification status persists in sessionStorage keyed by the signal, restoring it on mount if the stored value matches. A `handleWorldVerified` callback is being defined to finalize the flow.

The `handleWorldVerified` callback marks the reload as verified, saves the signal to sessionStorage, and advances the flow to step two. The reload button now either proceeds directly or opens the World ID gate based on verification status. Successful verification inside the gate also advances to step two. The API route for the RP signature endpoint is being implemented next.

The backend route validates the World ID signing key and RP ID. It defaults the action to `nuro-reload-verify` and calls `signRequest` to generate the signature, nonce, and expiration timestamp.

The signature endpoint strips the 0x prefix from the signing key before passing it to signRequest. It returns the RP ID, signature, nonce, and timestamps. The verify-proof API route is now being drafted.

The verify-proof API route validates the World ID configuration and extracts the IDKit result from the request body. It rejects requests without the required proof. To prevent client-side RP ID tampering, the server uses its own RP_ID environment variable. The route then forwards the proof to the World ID verification endpoint.

The verify-proof route now returns the World ID nullifier hash on success. The next step is installing the packages and checking types. A subtle verified indicator will appear above the reload button in ReloadOverview.

A muted "Verified with World ID" label will appear above the reload button when the user is authenticated. If the World ID app ID is missing, the gate will be skipped with a console warning so local development does not stall.</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Implementing World IDKit: API routes, gate component, and Reload flow integration.

_**Agent (default)**_

**Hard gate:** Wire World IDKit on Reload before step 2 (send funds).

**What changed:**

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `src/lib/world-id.ts` | Action + app ID helpers |
| `src/app/api/world/rp-signature/route.ts` | Signs RP context (`RP_SIGNING_KEY`) |
| `src/app/api/world/verify-proof/route.ts` | Forwards proof to World API |
| `src/features/dashboard/my-card-1/components/WorldIdReloadGate.tsx` | `IDKitRequestWidget` modal |
| `src/features/dashboard/my-card-1/components/ReloadFlow.tsx` | Gate on step 1 CTA |

**Flow:**
1. User enters amount on Reload step 1
2. CTA reads **"Verify & Reload …"** until World ID passes
3. Opens IDKit → backend verifies proof → advances to step 2 (QR/send)
4. Same session: **"Verified with World ID"** badge; CTA is plain **"Reload …"**

**Doppler env required:**

| Key | Value |
|-----|--------|
| `NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_ID` | `app_9e6da8c101719b64157f4fe86b174de7` |
| `RP_ID` | `rp_f1c9fb71634c144a` |
| `RP_SIGNING_KEY` | your private key |
| `NEXT_PUBLIC_WORLD_ACTION` | optional — defaults to `nuro-reload-verify` |

**Test:** Restart `pnpm dev` (picks up `NEXT_PUBLIC_*`), open Reload, enter amount, click **Verify & Reload**, complete World ID in the app.

If `NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_ID` is missing, the gate is skipped (dev fallback).

---

_**User**_

Scope lock

Owen/SD3 keys are dead — skip card reload / KYC for demo. Ship: World ID gate → Uniswap swap on Base (Privy signs) → show tx hash. Arc splitter + diagram is phase 3, not tonight unless IDKit + Uniswap are done.

World

Port from EthGlobal/world-minikit-js-main/demo/next-15-template: api/rp-signature, api/verify-proof, IDKit verify flow. Action name: [your action]. Env: NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_ID, RP_ID, RP_SIGNING_KEY in Doppler.

Uniswap (after IDKit)

Use .agents/skills/swap-integration + existing /api/quote/best in ReloadModal. Base only. Need tx hashes + feedback form for prize.

Don't touch

Richard monitor, LZ, PRIVATE_KEY deposit path. Hackathon branch only — no feat/responsive-home.

Reference

Security Refactor/ETHGlobal NY 2026 - hackathon.md for build order + submission blurbs.

If it's already wiring IDKit and not expanding scope, you don't need to say anything — let it run. Only interrupt if it starts on Arc first or touches Owen/card flows. What to do now
Restart FE (picks up NEXT_PUBLIC_*):
pnpm dev
Test: Reload → enter amount → Verify & Reload → complete World ID → should land on step 2 with “Verified with World ID” badge.
If gate doesn’t show, confirm Doppler has NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_ID, RP_ID, RP_SIGNING_KEY.
Optional in Doppler: NEXT_PUBLIC_WORLD_ACTION = your portal action name (defaults to nuro-reload-verify).

That Tooltip TypeScript error
Unrelated to World ID — recharts/generic tooltip typing somewhere else in the dashboard. Ignore for tonight unless pnpm build fails. Don’t let the agent chase it unless the dev server won’t start.

Next for the agent
World ID gate works. Step 2: Uniswap swap execution in Reload on Base (Privy signs). Use swap-integration skill + /api/quote/best. Need tx hash in UI for judges.

Arc + diagram after that.

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The hackathon flow starts with amount, token, and chain selection. Base is restricted for the swap path.

The swap UI replaces the Owen deposit QR. It fetches quotes from `/api/quote/best`, signs and submits the trade via Privy/wagmi, and displays the transaction hash. A new `useEvmSwapExecutor.ts` hook will handle EVM swaps using wagmi, following the existing dashboard pattern.

A dedicated `useBaseSwapExecutor.ts` hook will target Base chain 8453 and execute native ETH-to-USDC swaps via swap-firm. The `ReloadFlow` will branch after World ID verification: on Base, it routes to a new swap step instead of the Owen deposit QR screen. This replaces the existing step two behavior without restructuring the entire flow.

The swap path will trigger whenever World ID is configured, bypassing the Owen deposit QR entirely. Base chain and native ETH-to-USDC are hardcoded for this branch.

The swap path skips the Owen deposit monitor entirely. After a successful swap, the flow advances directly to the success screen and displays the transaction hash. The chain and token selectors will be restricted to Base and native ETH during this branch.

The swap UI will live in `ReloadSwapFunds.tsx`. It will use wagmi hooks for account state, transaction signing, and switching to Base chain 8453. Privy will provide wallet connection fallback. The executor mirrors the Jupiter pattern with `sendTransaction` and `swap-firm`. Constants are fixed at Base chain 8453 and native ETH as the sell token.

Restricting the first step to Base and native ETH would be too invasive for ReloadOverview. A cleaner approach is needed.

The first step will stay largely unchanged. The swap path activates when World ID is configured. The step one call-to-action changes to "Verify & Swap". The chain selector will be restricted to Base only under this mode. Native ETH is used as the sell token on Base. The amount field represents ETH quantity.

The swap UI will request live quotes from `/api/quote/best?chainId=8453&sellToken=native&amount=X`. The button will read "Connect Wallet" or "Sign & Swap". The executor hook must be a client component using wagmi hooks such as `useAccount`, `useSendTransaction`, and `useSwitchChain`.</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Scope locked. World ID + Uniswap swap on Base is wired. Owen/card deposit path untouched.

## Demo flow (when `NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_ID` is set)

1. **Step 1** — enter ETH amount → **Verify & Swap X ETH**
2. **World ID** — complete verification
3. **Step 2** — live quote via `/api/quote/best`, **Sign & Swap** (Privy wallet signs on Base)
4. **Success** — tx hash on screen + Basescan link

When World ID env is missing, legacy Owen QR path still runs (unchanged).

## New files

| File | Role |
|------|------|
| `hooks/useLiveSwapQuote.ts` | Debounced `/api/quote/best` |
| `hooks/useBaseSwapExecutor.ts` | Privy wallet → `/api/quote/swap-firm` → sign on Base |
| `components/ReloadSwapFunds.tsx` | Step 2 swap UI + tx hash |

## Test now

```bash
pnpm dev        # restart FE for NEXT_PUBLIC_*
pnpm dev:api    # BE :3000
```

1. Reload → amount → **Verify & Swap**
2. Complete World ID
3. **Connect Wallet** (Privy) if needed
4. **Sign & Swap** → confirm tx → hash on success screen

## Doppler checklist

- `NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_ID`, `RP_ID`, `RP_SIGNING_KEY`
- `NEXT_PUBLIC_PRIVY_APP_ID`
- `UNISWAP_API_KEY` (backend quotes)
- Optional: `NEXT_PUBLIC_WORLD_ACTION` (default `nuro-reload-verify`)

Arc + diagram = phase 3 after this path works end-to-end.

_**Agent (default)**_

Yes on git — push often on `hackathon/ethglobal-ny-2026`. Judges like commit history that shows real work during the event.

**DNS alone does not make it live.** GoDaddy only points the name at your server. Something on Hetzner has to be serving the app with HTTPS.

## What “live” actually means

```
ethglobal.nuro.finance  →  GoDaddy DNS  →  Hetzner IP
                                              ↓
                                    nginx/Caddy + SSL
                                              ↓
                              Next.js (FE) + Express (API) + Postgres
```

**Minimum on Hetzner before DNS:**

1. **App running** — build + run Next.js and the API (Docker, PM2, or similar)
2. **HTTPS** — Caddy or certbot/nginx + Let’s Encrypt (World portal wants `https://`)
3. **Postgres** — not your laptop Docker; DB on Hetzner or Neon/Supabase
4. **Env** — Doppler on server (`doppler setup` + `doppler run -- pnpm start`) or equivalent
5. **Production URLs in Doppler/Vercel-style env:**
   - `NEXTAUTH_URL=https://ethglobal.nuro.finance`
   - `NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL` / `BACKEND_URL` → API URL (e.g. `https://api.ethglobal.nuro.finance` or same host with reverse proxy)

## GoDaddy (after the server answers)

| Type | Name | Value |
|------|------|--------|
| **A** | `ethglobal` | Hetzner server IP |

Optional API subdomain: `api` → same IP, nginx routes `/api` or separate vhost.

DNS can take 5–30 min. SSL needs the app listening on 80/443 first.

## Simpler alternative for the weekend

**Vercel (FE) + Railway/Render (API + DB)** is often faster than hand-rolling Hetzner for a Next + Express monorepo. Hetzner is fine if you already know the stack — just not “DNS flip only.”

## Practical order during the event

1. Commit + push frequently on hackathon branch  
2. Smoke test locally (World → swap → tx hash)  
3. Deploy to Hetzner (or Vercel) **first**  
4. Confirm `https://ethglobal.nuro.finance` loads in a browser  
5. **Then** point GoDaddy A record  
6. Update World App URL + `NEXTAUTH_URL` to production  

**Bottom line:** Git activity = branch pushes. Going live = deploy on Hetzner + SSL + DB + env, **then** DNS. If you want, we can sketch a minimal Hetzner layout (nginx + PM2 + Doppler) for this repo next session.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

_**Agent (default)**_

Neon is a good fit — use it for Postgres so you don’t run a DB on Hetzner.

## Stack that makes sense

| Piece | Where |
|-------|--------|
| **Postgres** | Neon |
| **FE** (Next.js :2800) | Hetzner or Vercel |
| **API** (Express :3000) | Hetzner (same box or separate) |
| **Secrets** | Doppler (`nuro-ethglobal` / `dev` or a `prod` config) |

## Neon setup (5 min)

1. Create a project in Neon (e.g. `nuro-ethglobal`)
2. Copy the connection string → looks like  
   `postgresql://user:pass@ep-xxx.region.aws.neon.tech/neondb?sslmode=require`
3. Put in Doppler (hackathon deploy config):
   - `DATABASE_URL`
   - `POSTGRES_URL` (same value if your app uses both)
4. Run migrations against Neon once from your laptop:
   ```bash
   cd "/Users/cjb369/Documents/1. nuro-finance-local/nuro-ethglobal-hackathon"
   doppler run -- pnpm db:migrate   # or your migrate script
   ```

Local Docker Postgres stays for dev; Neon is for **deployed** API only.

## On Hetzner

API only needs the Neon URL in env — no Postgres container on the server.

```bash
doppler run -- pnpm dev:api   # locally
# on server:
doppler run -- tsx src/index.ts   # or your production start command
```

Point `BACKEND_URL` / `NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL` at whatever public URL serves the API (`https://api.ethglobal.nuro.finance` or proxied under the main domain).

## Doppler tip

Optional: add a **`prod`** or **`ethglobal`** config in the same project with Neon URLs + production `NEXTAUTH_URL`, keep **`dev`** for local. Same keys, different values.

**Bottom line:** Neon = DB for deploy. Hetzner = app processes + nginx/SSL. GoDaddy = DNS after HTTPS works. You’re not missing anything critical on the DB side.

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Evaluating whether Vercel is the right deployment choice for this ETHGlobal hackathon setup.

Vercel is ideal for the Next.js frontend. The Express API cannot run natively on Vercel as a long-running server. This creates a major architectural conflict for the full stack.

Vercel should host only the Next.js frontend. The Express API must run elsewhere, such as Railway, Render, Fly, or Hetzner. Serverless API routes would not cover the full backend because the application proxies many routes to localhost:3000.</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

**Vercel for the frontend — yes.** For the **whole app — no**, unless you only need the Next.js part.

## Your stack is split

| Process | What it is | Vercel? |
|---------|------------|--------|
| `pnpm dev` | Next.js (FE) | ✅ Native |
| `pnpm dev:api` | Express (`src/index.ts`) | ❌ Long-running server, not a Vercel fit |
| DB | Neon | ✅ Either way |

Reload, World ID routes, and `/api/*` proxies assume a **live API** at `BACKEND_URL` (port 3000 locally). That doesn’t run on Vercel as-is.

## Best weekend combo (my pick)

```
Vercel     →  ethglobal.nuro.finance     (Next.js)
Railway or Hetzner  →  api.ethglobal.nuro.finance  (Express)
Neon       →  Postgres
Doppler    →  secrets (sync to Vercel + API host)
```

**Why Vercel for FE**
- Push branch → deploy in minutes  
- SSL + custom domain (GoDaddy CNAME → Vercel)  
- `NEXT_PUBLIC_*` env at build time  
- Less to debug than nginx on Hetzner during the event  

**Why not Vercel-only**
- You’d still need API + DB somewhere for login, quotes, swap-firm, World verify routes that hit the backend  

**Hetzner still useful**
- Run **only the API** there if you don’t want Railway  
- Or skip Hetzner for the weekend and use Railway/Fly for API (often faster to ship)

## If you want one box only

All-in on **Hetzner** (FE + API + nginx) is fine if you’re comfortable with it — just more setup than Vercel for Next.js.

## Practical call

| Priority | Choice |
|----------|--------|
| Ship fast, judge URL live | **Vercel (FE) + Railway or Hetzner (API) + Neon** |
| You already know Hetzner well | **Hetzner for both** — skip Vercel |
| Minimize moving parts | **Vercel FE + Railway API** — Hetzner optional |

I’d do **Vercel for `ethglobal.nuro.finance`** and put the API on **Railway** (quick) or **your Hetzner box** (free, more hands-on). Neon stays the DB either way.

Want a step-by-step for Vercel + Railway + Doppler env list for this repo?

Other options include exporting only a subdirectory or keeping the main app private while publishing a stripped-down fork. ETHGlobal typically wants commit activity during the event rather than full Nuro history. Pushing the branch as-is risks exposing the entire codebase and internal commit messages.

The worktree branch contains uncommitted changes and possibly secrets or `.specstory` files. The safest path is to create an orphan `main` branch on the public repo and squash only the current hackathon worktree files. Cherry-picking existing commits is not viable since no new commits exist yet.

The hackathon branch sits at the same commit as the main Nuro app, so pushing it would expose the entire monorepo. _**Agent (default)**_

**Don’t push your linked Nuro history to the public repo.** Best practice here is a **separate public snapshot**, not your real app’s git lineage.

## The risk

`hackathon/ethglobal-ny-2026` shares history with `feat/responsive-home` at `7e0a57e`. A normal push would publish:

- The **whole app tree** at that commit (not just World ID / swap)
- **All prior commit messages** and file history
- Anything ever committed by mistake (env snippets, internal docs, etc.)

Orphan fixes **history**; it does **not** hide files you include in the snapshot. If you copy the full worktree, the public repo still contains the full codebase as files — just with one clean commit.

## Best practice (what most teams do)

| Keep private | Publish publicly |
|--------------|------------------|
| `Nuro-Finance` repo / real remotes | `Eth_Global_Hackathon` only |
| Full history, internal branches | **Orphan `main`** — 1+ hackathon commits |
| Doppler secrets | Code only (no `.env`, no `.specstory`) |

**Flow:** keep developing in the worktree → when you publish, **export a clean snapshot** to the public repo → keep committing there during the event.

## Recommended approach

**1. New orphan `main` for GitHub only** (no parent commits):

```bash
cd "/Users/cjb369/Documents/1. nuro-finance-local/nuro-ethglobal-hackathon"

# commit your local hackathon work first (on hackathon branch, stays local/private)
# ... your commit ...

# create orphan publish branch
git checkout --orphan ethglobal-public
git add -A   # AFTER scrubbing — see below
git commit -m "chore(hackathon): initial public submission scaffold"
```

**2. Scrub before `git add`** (public for 3 weeks):

- ❌ `.specstory/`, `.env*`, any credentials JSON  
- ❌ Internal-only docs if you don’t want them public (`docs/Claude Memory/`, etc.)  
- ✅ `doppler.yaml` (no secrets in it)  
- Run: `git grep -iE 'sk_|api_key|private_key|mnemonic|0x[a-f0-9]{64}'` on staged files  

**3. Push only to the hackathon remote:**

```bash
git remote add ethglobal https://github.com/Nuro-Finance/Eth_Global_Hackathon.git
git push -u ethglobal ethglobal-public:main
```

Your real app never gets an `origin` pointing at this public repo.

**4. During the event:** commit + `git push ethglobal` often (judges see activity on **public** history only).

**5. After ~3 weeks:** archive the repo, make private if your plan allows, or delete — real app stays in private Nuro repo.

## What ETHGlobal actually needs

- Public repo with **hackathon-period commits**  
- Runnable demo / README / video  
- They do **not** need your full internal git graph

## Practical call

| Option | Verdict |
|--------|---------|
| Push `hackathon/ethglobal-ny-2026` as-is | ❌ Leaks Nuro history |
| Orphan `main` + scrubbed snapshot | ✅ Standard approach |
| Minimal repo (only hackathon paths) | ✅ Best privacy, more work |

**Tonight:** orphan snapshot + scrub is enough. **Tomorrow:** you can trim to a smaller public tree if you want less surface area.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The user wants to confirm whether those commands create an orphan branch and if they should run them in their terminal.

`--orphan` creates a branch with no parent commits. `git add -A` must not run before scrubbing sensitive files. The hackathon branch should be committed first to preserve private history.

The `--orphan` command creates a new branch with no commits. The working tree files carry over from the current branch. The first commit on the orphan becomes its root commit. The original hackathon branch remains intact with full history.</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Yes — `git checkout --orphan ethglobal-public` creates an **orphan branch**: no parent commits. The first commit on it is the **root** of a new history (what you push to the public repo).

**Don’t run that block verbatim yet** — `git add -A` before scrubbing would include `.specstory/` and anything else you don’t want public.

## Safer sequence (run in Terminal)

**1. Commit hackathon work on your private branch** (local only, not pushed to public):

```bash
cd "/Users/cjb369/Documents/1. nuro-finance-local/nuro-ethglobal-hackathon"

git checkout hackathon/ethglobal-ny-2026

git add doppler.yaml package.json pnpm-lock.yaml \
  src/app/api/world/ \
  src/features/dashboard/my-card-1/components/ReloadFlow.tsx \
  src/features/dashboard/my-card-1/components/ReloadSwapFunds.tsx \
  src/features/dashboard/my-card-1/components/WorldIdReloadGate.tsx \
  src/features/dashboard/my-card-1/hooks/ \
  src/lib/world-id.ts

git commit -m "chore(hackathon): Doppler, World ID gate, Base swap reload scaffold"
```

**2. Orphan branch** (new history, no old commits):

```bash
git checkout --orphan ethglobal-public
```

You’re on a branch with **zero commits**. The working tree still has your files.

**3. Stage only what should be public** (exclude `.specstory`):

```bash
git add doppler.yaml package.json pnpm-lock.yaml src/ public/ scripts/ \
  contracts/ admin/ cli/ nuro-vaults/ \
  # add other dirs you need for the demo — or use git add with care

# DO NOT: git add .specstory .specstory/
```

Or, if the whole app tree is fine **except** junk:

```bash
git add -A
git reset HEAD .specstory .cursorindexingignore   # unstage if present
```

**4. First (orphan) commit:**

```bash
git commit -m "chore(hackathon): initial public submission scaffold"
```

That commit has **no parents** → orphan history.

**5. Push to GitHub only:**

```bash
git remote add ethglobal https://github.com/Nuro-Finance/Eth_Global_Hackathon.git
# skip if remote already exists

git push -u ethglobal ethglobal-public:main
```

**6. Go back to daily dev:**

```bash
git checkout hackathon/ethglobal-ny-2026
```

---

## What orphan does / doesn’t do

| | |
|--|--|
| ✅ | Public repo gets **one clean root commit** — no old Nuro git history |
| ✅ | Old branch `hackathon/ethglobal-ny-2026` **still exists locally** with full history |
| ❌ | Does **not** hide files in the snapshot — full codebase is still visible as **files** |
| ❌ | Does **not** replace scrubbing — still exclude `.specstory`, secrets, internal docs you don’t want public |

**Bottom line:** run it in your terminal, but commit on `hackathon/ethglobal-ny-2026` first, then `--orphan`, then **selective** `git add` (not blind `-A`), then push to `ethglobal` only.

**Two steps — not “commit local → push that commit to public.”**

## Step 1 — Commit local (private, stays on your machine)

```bash
git checkout hackathon/ethglobal-ny-2026
git add … 
git commit -m "chore(hackathon): Doppler, World ID gate, Base swap reload scaffold"
```

**Purpose:** Save hackathon work on your real branch. **Do not** `git push` this branch to `Eth_Global_Hackathon`.

Your old Nuro history stays local unless you push it somewhere.

---

## Step 2 — Orphan + push (this is what goes public)

```bash
git checkout --orphan ethglobal-public
git add …   # scrub first, exclude .specstory
git commit -m "chore(hackathon): initial public submission scaffold"

git remote add ethglobal https://github.com/Nuro-Finance/Eth_Global_Hackathon.git
git push -u ethglobal ethglobal-public:main
```

**Purpose:** GitHub gets **one new root commit** — no prior Nuro history.

---

## Flow

```
hackathon/ethglobal-ny-2026  →  commit tonight (local backup / keep building)
         ↓
ethglobal-public (orphan)    →  one clean commit → push to Eth_Global_Hackathon
```

During the hackathon: keep working on `hackathon/ethglobal-ny-2026`, then periodically refresh public with new orphan commits or cherry-pick/squash — simplest is commit on hackathon branch, then re-orphan or add commits on `ethglobal-public` and push. Actually for ongoing work during event they might:
- Option A: work on ethglobal-public after initial orphan, push there during event (loses link to private branch commits but public history is clean incremental commits)
- Option B: work on hackathon branch locally, squash/export to public periodically

For simplicity during event: after first orphan push, they could **keep committing on `ethglobal-public`** and push — each new commit still won't include OLD nuro history, only builds on the orphan root.

Or switch back to hackathon branch for dev and only push ethglobal-public when they want to update public.

I'll keep the answer simple for the user's question: yes commit local first, then separate orphan push to public - not push step 1 directly.
**Yes — commit local first. No — don’t push that commit straight to the public repo.**

| Step | Branch | Push to GitHub? |
|------|--------|-----------------|
| 1. `git commit` on `hackathon/ethglobal-ny-2026` | Your private hackathon branch | **No** — local only |
| 2. `git checkout --orphan ethglobal-public` + commit + push | Clean public history | **Yes** → `Eth_Global_Hackathon` |

Step 1 = backup so you don’t lose work.  
Step 2 = what judges see (orphan `main`, no old Nuro git history).

After step 2, go back to `hackathon/ethglobal-ny-2026` for day-to-day dev. When you want to update GitHub during the event, add commits on `ethglobal-public` and `git push ethglobal` again — still no leaked old history.

You have two worktrees, same git repo, different branches:

| Folder | Branch | What a commit here does |
|--------|--------|---------------------------|
| `nuro-ethglobal-hackathon` | `hackathon/ethglobal-ny-2026` | Moves **only** this branch forward |
| `Nuro-Finance` | `feat/responsive-home` | Untouched — stays on its current commit |

`git commit` on the hackathon branch does **not**:
- Update `feat/responsive-home`
- Touch production
- Push anywhere (you have no `origin` yet — it’s local only)

`feat/responsive-home` would only pick up hackathon work if you explicitly **merge** or **cherry-pick** into it. Don’t do that for hackathon stuff.

**Summary:** Commit in the ethglobal worktree = safe, hackathon-only, local until you push to the public orphan repo.
